content:
After the commit 50384460c68f ("Rewrite method resolution to follow rustc more closely") to rust-analyzer (included in release 2025-11-24 (v0.3.2693)), our current rust-project.json setup does not provide proper IDE support for inherent methods of primitive types:

fn foo() {
    // rust-analyzer does not provide LSP features like autocomplete,
    // go-to-definition, etc. on `rotate_left`.
    let _ = 0_i32.rotate_left(0);
}

This is because the method resolution logic has changed. A detailed description of the root cause can be found in this thread [1].

To fix this issue without touching the rust-analyzer side, I think the following steps are needed:

  1. Re-enable sysroot_src in rust-project.json.
  2. Remove -Zcrate-attr=no_std from scripts/Makefile.build.
  3. Explicitly add #![no_std] to all kernel modules.

... or simply downgrade rust-analyzer.

Appreciate any thoughts on this issue. Specifically, I'm curious about why the unstable -Zcrate-attr=no_std is used instead of #![no_std].

[1] https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/185405-t-compiler.2Frust-analyzer/topic/Primitive.20type.20inherent.20method.20lookup.20fails/with/561607963

Thanks Jesung, very appreciated that you took a good look at this.

To fix this issue without touching the rust-analyzer side, I think the following steps are needed:

Yeah, we cannot really do that (I mean, everything is possible, but it would be a major shift), and -Zcrate-attr will likely be stabilized anyway.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Yeah, we cannot really do that (I mean, everything is possible, but it would be a major shift), and -Zcrate-attr will likely be stabilized anyway.

I see. Since we're moving toward supporting -Zcrate-attr on the rust-analyzer side, I think the only change we need here is explicitly setting sysroot_src.
If there's going to be a new option in the json for -Zcrate-attr, we probably need to add those too.
Oh, I missed that one. Thanks for pointing it out.

Possibly unrelated but I just noticed that resolving .iter() on types where that method is not inherent no longer works for me (and still doesn't after applying https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/6dbdf6e1c1639ae381ca9ab7041f84728ffa2267.1764062688.git.y.j3ms.n@gmail.com/.

Do you folks see that as well?
IIRC, there's no .iter() trait method in std or syn. Could you provide an example?
You're right, It's not a trait method, it is an inherent method on &[T]
In fact it didn't work for me even when I did the deref from Vec to slice manually. So it seems to be an issue with inherent slice methods?
I think it is, since slice is also a primitive type.
Right, but the patch above did not fix it for me. Does it fix it for you?

The fix for inherent method resolution was sent as a different patch: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260101-ra-fix-primitive-v1-1-def809357b4e@gmail.com/

Tamir Duberstein said:

Right, but the patch above did not fix it for me.

Yeah, this is the exact reason why I started this thread :)

FWIW, I believe we're moving toward supporting multiple rust-analyzer versions [1]. Since the fix requires a quite recent rust-analyzer release (2025-12-22, v0.3.2727), we likely need to account for that multi-version support first.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CA+tqQ4+SVQfDmtg5nE0E-staohkHaiZ9en7YK7tJ=S=AkF6=fQ@mail.gmail.com/
